skill.name: uspto-database
skill.description: Access USPTO patent, trademark, examination, assignment, citation, and office action data for IP research and prior art analysis.
skill.summary: Search USPTO patent and trademark data with API references and Python helper clients.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown code fences, USPTO legal terminology, and API documentation. Confirmed risks are legitimate outbound network requests with API-key handling and one command-line API-key option; no evidence found of malicious exfiltration, prompt override, or automatic shell execution. Publish with a warning that the skill runs community scripts and contacts USPTO or PatentsView services.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Outbound USPTO API Requests
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Python clients and examples that send API keys in request headers to USPTO and PatentsView endpoints. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it requires user approval because it combines outbound network access with credential handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls and API-key headers are directly present in scripts and documentation. The destinations appear to be USPTO or PatentsView services, so I did not find evidence of malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: API Key Can Be Passed on Command Line
medium_findings.description: The trademark helper accepts an API key through a command-line flag. This is convenient, but command-line secrets can be retained in shell history or visible to local process inspection; environment variables are safer for routine use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The --api-key option is explicit and is passed into the client. The impact depends on local execution practices, so this is a moderate handling risk rather than evidence of hostile behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Trigger External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, curl examples, and installation examples. I did not find automatic shell execution in the Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited patterns are Markdown examples or documentation syntax. The executable Python files do not call subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec in the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Windows SAM Alerts Are Domain-Term False Positives
low_findings.description: The blocker findings are caused by patent statute references, field names, office action codes, and the legal term SECURITY AGREEMENT. These are USPTO domain terms, not cryptographic code or Windows credential database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious words appear in legal and patent-examination context. No evidence found of hashing functions, cryptographic choices, or access to Windows SAM files.

low_findings.title: Promotional Behavioral Instruction in Skill Text
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not a security override, but it can bias model behavior toward an external hosted service outside the core USPTO task.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and unrelated to querying USPTO data. It does not contain classic prompt-injection phrases such as ignoring prior instructions, so the severity remains low.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated Outbound USPTO API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Python clients and examples that send API keys in request headers to USPTO and PatentsView endpoints. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it requires user approval because it combines outbound network access with credential handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Network calls and API-key headers are directly present in scripts and documentation. The destinations appear to be USPTO or PatentsView services, so I did not find evidence of malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Can Be Passed on Command Line
dangerous_patterns.description: The trademark helper accepts an API key through a command-line flag. This is convenient, but command-line secrets can be retained in shell history or visible to local process inspection; environment variables are safer for routine use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The --api-key option is explicit and is passed into the client. The impact depends on local execution practices, so this is a moderate handling risk rather than evidence of hostile behavior.

content.user_title: Search USPTO Patent and Trademark Data
content.value_statement: Patent and trademark research often requires several USPTO systems and careful query syntax. This skill provides API guidance, helper scripts, and workflow patterns for faster IP analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to search granted patents by keyword, inventor, assignee, classification, and date.
Provides Python helper clients for PatentSearch, PEDS, and trademark status retrieval.
Shows how to retrieve patent examination history, office actions, and prosecution summaries.
Documents trademark lookup by serial number, registration number, ownership, and prosecution history.
Covers USPTO assignment, citation, litigation, PTAB, and office action data sources.
Combines multiple USPTO sources into portfolio and prior art analysis workflows.
limitations: Most live API workflows require USPTO or PatentsView API keys.
Some text endpoints are beta or have incomplete historical coverage.
The PEDS helper depends on an external Python package for full operation.
The skill supports research workflows and does not provide legal advice.

use_cases.title: Prior Art Search
use_cases.description: Find related patents by keyword, CPC class, inventor, assignee, and date before drafting a patentability memo.

use_cases.title: Trademark Status Review
use_cases.description: Check trademark status, goods and services, owners, and prosecution events for active applications or registrations.

use_cases.title: Portfolio Monitoring
use_cases.description: Track assignment records, citation activity, prosecution events, and litigation signals across a company portfolio.

prompt_templates.title: Find Patents by Topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search USPTO patent data for patents about [technology]. Focus on abstracts, recent grant dates, and major assignees.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Patent
prompt_templates.prompt: Retrieve details for patent [number]. Summarize title, abstract, inventors, assignees, grant date, CPC classes, and citations.

prompt_templates.title: Check Trademark Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Look up trademark serial or registration number [number]. Report current status, owner details, goods and services, and prosecution concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Build IP Portfolio Brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the USPTO portfolio for [company]. Combine patent search, assignments, prosecution history, citations, and trademark records into a concise brief.

output_examples.input: Search recent machine learning patents assigned to a named company.
output_examples.output: A ranked patent list with patent numbers, titles, grant dates, assignees, short abstracts, and suggested follow-up filters.

output_examples.input: Review a trademark serial number for current status.
output_examples.output: A status summary with mark text, filing date, registration details, owner information, goods and services, and attention items.

output_examples.input: Create a portfolio overview for a competitor.
output_examples.output: A research brief covering patent families, active assignees, assignment changes, citation patterns, prosecution issues, and data gaps.

best_practices: Store API keys in environment variables and avoid command-line secrets.
Request only needed fields and respect published rate limits.
Verify important findings against official USPTO records before legal or business decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not treat API output as legal advice or a freedom-to-operate opinion.
Do not run broad automated searches without rate limiting and caching.
Do not publish API keys, raw credentials, or confidential search targets in prompts.

faq.question: Does this skill require API keys?
faq.answer: Yes. Most live USPTO and PatentsView API workflows require an API key.

faq.question: Can it search both patents and trademarks?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers PatentSearch, PEDS, TSDR, and assignment-related USPTO sources.

faq.question: Does it provide legal advice?
faq.answer: No. It supports data gathering and analysis, but legal conclusions require qualified counsel.

faq.question: Can it retrieve prosecution history?
faq.answer: Yes. The PEDS helper and documentation support examination history and office action workflows.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The skill includes community scripts that make authenticated network requests to USPTO services.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
